essence: David and the Chiang Mai crew (Kevin, Martin, Nick, Ian) commit in principle to a weekend hackathon building a dedicated AI macro keyboard for Claude Code vibe coding — proof-of-concept on StreamDecks, custom-printed shell designed with Nano Banana, then a Kickstarter leveraging Don (crowdfunding veteran) and Dana (China manufacturing).

actions.text: Run a weekend hackathon to proof-of-concept the AI macro keyboard on existing StreamDecks (David loaning his small unit), then design custom hardware and launch a Kickstarter

essence: David and Kevin explore Nano Banana contextual data visualizations via structured JSON prompts — Kevin's Nate B Jones-inspired workflow (transcript + timestamps into Claude, translation-style prompt to replicate techniques) aimed at parsing large wearable-recording volumes to surface knowledge gaps like leadership and communication.

POEM story 3.3 slide-deck sync: guidance scoped only to files changing now (3.4 handled later), include actual regression-tool output in slides for concrete demos, and open question of when the 3.3 deck is finished so a 3.4 deck can start.
David approves the decisions made so far and green-lights the Scrum Master agent to create and update all relevant stories within epics ('whatever you said there, do it all').

Workflow schema design for epic 4: workflow level is a shared key-value attribute store (not separate input/output/transient concepts) fed by prompt nodes, API nodes and human-in-the-loop steps, with optional mapper objects translating generic prompt field names to workflow field names; epic 4 stories (4.2/4.3/4.5/4.6) need changes but detailed decisions deferred until schemas stabilize.

Engineer agent notes that supporting evidence for prompt schema work exists in the data folder's YouTube launch optimizer outputs — read prompt plus output together since outputs carry field names without schema info.

Output schemas recognized as the missing 'other half' of the prompt — current human-readable title tables are just strings, not programmatic structure (should be array/CSV/JSONL), and the Epic 4 workflow dataset must hold three data types: normalized inputs, normalized outputs, and transient human-in-the-loop values never sent to the model.

Decision that prompt-level schema work (templates, placeholders, typed output for prompt-to-attribute-store mapping) belongs in Epic 3, not deferred to Epic 4, with BMAD agents (PO first, then architect and SM) brought in for course correction in a fresh context.

actions.text: Create a task list covering product owner, architect, and Scrum Master perspectives for the epic 3 schema course-correction

essence: Live POEM demo of the YouTube-title prompt (Penny agent, Handlebars conditional context, refine command, new list/view commands): settled on a table output surfacing emotion, character count, mobile flag, keywords and title, and crystallized that a prompt's schema comprises both input AND output — prompts are functions — which may require story/slide updates.

Refine command semantics settled: refine fixes the prompt template, not the outcome — the output only diagnoses template problems (e.g. repeated 'twenty minutes') — with full proper implementation deferred to story 3.6 plus interim one-liner list/view additions to the prompt engineer.

actions.text: Implement the refine command properly (preview with data, generate schema) in story 3.6, adding one-liner list and view commands to the prompt engineer beforehand

essence: Loom demo of the universal starter script launching four FliVideo apps (FliHub, FliGen, FliDeck, Storyline) on separate ports with PO/dev Claude conversations per app, plus BMAD too-much-data advice (hallucinations/prompt poisoning countered by visualization + disciplined human-in-the-loop) — and note that David joined Brian's BMAD team in an ideas/feedback capacity.

Universal project starter: a small standardized starter script in each project root plus a global 's' shell alias presenting numbered start options (npm/bun/docker/multi-service), with a comprehensive starter guide being written to share with the Skool community, using SupportSignal (BMAD v4) as the complex example.

actions.text: Finish the comprehensive project starter guide and publish it to the Skool community
